session.user: I want to look into converting the docs about the tui in @docs/learned and reorganizing them as a skill
session.assistant: Let me explore the TUI documentation first.

session.assistant: That's a lot of docs — 37 TUI files + 7 Textual files. Let me read the indexes and a sample of the docs to understand their content and structure.
session.tool_result:
1→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 2→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 3→ 4→# Tui Documentation 5→ 6→- **[action-inventory.md](action-inventory.md)** — adding a new command to the TUI or desktop dashboard, understanding how command availability is determined, choosing which execution pattern a new command should use 7→- **[adding-commands.md](adding-commands.md)** — adding a new command to the TUI command palette, implementing TUI actions with streaming output, understanding the dual-handler pattern for TUI commands 8→- **[architecture.md](architecture.md)** — understanding TUI structure, implementing TUI components, working with TUI data providers 9→- **[async-action-refresh-pattern.md](async-action-refresh-pattern.md)** — adding background operations to the TUI, implementing worker thread actions in the dashboard, refreshing TUI data after a background subprocess 10→- **[async-state-snapshot.md](async-state-snapshot.md)** — adding async data fetching to the TUI, reading self.\_view_mode during async operations, debugging stale data appearing in the wrong tab 11→- **[clipboard-text-generation.md](clipboard-text-generation.md)** — implementing copy-to-clipboard in the TUI, adding a new command that supports clipboard copy, debugging why a copy command returns wrong text 12→- **[column-addition-pattern.md](column-addition-pattern.md)** — adding a column to the plan table, adding a field to PlanRowData, modifying plan_table.py column layout 13→- **[command-execution.md](command-execution.md)** — executing commands in TUI, choosing between sync and streaming execution, implementing command runners 14→- **[command-palette.md](command-palette.md)** — implementing command palette in Textual TUI, hiding system commands from command palette, get_system_commands method, removing Keys Quit Screenshot Theme from palette, adding emoji prefixes to command palette entries, using CommandCategory for command categorization 15→- **[dashboard-columns.md](dashboard-columns.md)** — adding a new column to the erk dash TUI, understanding which columns are always present vs conditional, debugging why a column is missing in a particular view mode 16→- **[data-contract.md](data-contract.md)** — building an alternate frontend consuming plan data, adding fields to PlanRowData or PlanDataProvider, understanding the display-vs-raw field duality, serializing plan data to JSON for external consumers 17→- **[derived-display-columns.md](derived-display-columns.md)** — adding a TUI column that uses an existing PlanRowData field, deciding whether a new column needs gateway/query changes 18→- **[dual-handler-pattern.md](dual-handler-pattern.md)** — implementing a TUI command that works from both list and detail views, understanding how MainListCommandProvider and PlanCommandProvider share commands, adding command palette support to a new screen 19→- **[filter-pipeline.md](filter-pipeline.md)** — adding a new filter to the TUI dashboard, understanding how objective/stack/text filters interact, modifying the escape key behavior in the TUI 20→- **[filter-toggle-pattern.md](filter-toggle-pattern.md)** — adding a filter toggle to the TUI dashboard, implementing server-side filtering in the TUI, adding keybindings that toggle dashboard state 21→- **[frozen-dataclass-field-management.md](frozen-dataclass-field-management.md)** — removing a field from a frozen dataclass, renaming a field in PlanRowData or similar frozen dataclass, getting unexpected constructor errors after field changes 22→- **[keyboard-shortcuts.md](keyboard-shortcuts.md)** — adding a new keyboard shortcut to the TUI, checking for shortcut conflicts before binding a new key, understanding what keys are available in the TUI 23→- **[lifecycle-display.md](lifecycle-display.md)** — adding a new lifecycle stage to the TUI, changing lifecycle abbreviations or colors, modifying PR status emoji indicators, understanding the stage column in erk dash 24→- **[modal-screen-pattern.md](modal-screen-pattern.md)** — adding a new modal screen to the TUI, implementing a ModalScreen subclass, displaying detail views or confirmation dialogs 25→- **[modal-widget-embedding.md](modal-widget-embedding.md)** — reusing PlanDataTable in a modal screen, embedding complex widgets in Textual modal screens, handling null safety for optional gateway fields in TUI 26→- **[multi-operation-tracking.md](multi-operation-tracking.md)** — adding a new background operation to the TUI, implementing status bar progress for a workflow command, debugging operation lifecycle or stuck operations 27→- **[one-shot-prompt-modal.md](one-shot-prompt-modal.md)** — adding a new modal or input screen to the TUI, working with the one-shot dispatch workflow in erk dash, adding global keybindings that trigger modals 28→- **[plan-row-data.md](plan-row-data.md)** — writing command availability predicates, understanding what data is available for TUI commands, checking which PlanRowData fields are nullable 29→- **[plan-title-rendering-pipeline.md](plan-title-rendering-pipeline.md)** — debugging why plan titles display incorrectly, troubleshooting missing prefixes in TUI, understanding plan data flow in TUI 30→- **[runs-tab-architecture.md](runs-tab-architecture.md)** — working with TUI Runs tab, adding columns to RunDataTable, understanding branch resolution in runs, modifying workflow run display in TUI
... [11 lines omitted] ...

1→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 2→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 3→ 4→# Textual Documentation 5→ 6→- **[background-workers.md](background-workers.md)** — running subprocess in TUI without blocking, streaming output to Textual widgets, using run_worker for async operations 7→- **[datatable-markup-escaping.md](datatable-markup-escaping.md)** — adding cell values to Textual DataTable, displaying user data in DataTable cells, troubleshooting invisible text in DataTable 8→- **[quirks.md](quirks.md)** — working with Textual TUI framework, debugging DataTable, App, or CSS issues in Textual, writing tests for Textual applications 9→- **[testing.md](testing.md)** — testing Textual TUI components, writing tests for ModalScreen, testing keyboard bindings, using Pilot API for async tests 10→- **[widget-development.md](widget-development.md)** — creating Textual widgets, adding ModalScreen dialogs, implementing keyboard bindings, writing Textual CSS styles 11→

1→--- 2→title: Tui Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on tui code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from tui/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Tui Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**accessing \_status_bar without null guard** → Read [TUI Streaming Output Patterns](streaming-output.md) first. Guard \_status_bar access with `if self._status_bar is not None:` — timing issue during widget lifecycle can cause AttributeError. 16→ 17→**accessing optional gateway fields without null checks in TUI event handlers** → Read [Modal Widget Embedding Pattern](modal-widget-embedding.md) first. Gateway fields like plan_body or objective_content may be None. Always check before accessing in event handlers. 18→ 19→**adding CI check formatting to TUI screens** → Read [TUI Architecture Overview](architecture.md) first. Use src/erk/tui/formatting/ci_checks.py shared module (format_check_line, format_summary_blockquote, format_check_runs) instead of duplicating formatting logic. 20→ 21→**adding a CLI flag that affects behavior without checking TUI command palette** → Read [TUI Command Registration](tui-command-registration.md) first. TUI command palette generates shell commands via src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py. When adding CLI flags that change behavior, check if TUI-generated commands need the flag too. 22→ 23→**adding a DataTable column with add_column(key=...)** → Read [TUI Architecture Overview](architecture.md) first. Column key is a data binding contract — must match data field name. Silent failure when mismatched. 24→ 25→**adding a column to PlanDataTable without updating make_plan_row** → Read [Column Addition Pattern](column-addition-pattern.md) first. Column additions require 5 coordinated changes. See column-addition-pattern.md for the complete checklist. 26→ 27→**adding a command without an availability predicate** → Read [TUI Command Architecture](action-inventory.md) first. Every command needs an is_available predicate based on PlanRowData field presence. Commands without predicates appear when they can't execute. 28→ 29→**adding a field to PlanRowData without updating make_plan_row** → Read [TUI Data Contract](data-contract.md) first. The fake's make_plan_row() helper must stay in sync. Add the new field with a sensible default there too, or all TUI tests will break. 30→
... [110 lines omitted] ...
35→**adding a new ViewMode without updating VIEW_CONFIGS** → Read [TUI View Switching](view-switching.md) first. Every ViewMode must have a corresponding ViewConfig in VIEW_CONFIGS. Missing configs cause KeyError at runtime. 45→**adding a subprocess call to the TUI without stderr inspection** → Read [TUI Subprocess Feedback Patterns](subprocess-feedback.md) first. TUI subprocess calls should inspect stderr for known success/failure markers. See subprocess-feedback.md for the pattern. 65→**calling start_operation() without a matching finish_operation() in the error path** → Read [TUI Multi-Operation Tracking](multi-operation-tracking.md) first. Always call finish_operation() in both success and error paths. Use try/finally or explicit error handling. Missing finish calls leave ghost operations in the status bar. 75→**constructing PlanFilters without copying all fields from existing filters** → Read [TUI Data Contract](data-contract.md) first. All fields must be explicitly copied in \_load_data() PlanFilters construction. Missing fields (like creator) cause silent filtering failures. 89→**extending PlanDataProvider ABC** → Read [TUI Architecture Overview](architecture.md) first. Requires 3-file update: abc.py + real.py + fake.py. Fake must initialize new dict in **init**. Missing init causes AttributeError at test time. 117→**removing a field from a frozen dataclass** → Read [Frozen Dataclass Field Management](frozen-dataclass-field-management.md) first. Grep for the class name across ALL constructor sites. Frozen dataclasses have 5+ places to update: field definition, real provider, fake provider, test helpers, and filtering/display logic. Missing one causes runtime TypeError.

1→--- 2→title: Textual Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on textual code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from textual/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Textual Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**adding cell values to Textual DataTable** → Read [DataTable Rich Markup Escaping](datatable-markup-escaping.md) first. Always wrap in `Text(value)` if strings contain user data with brackets. Otherwise `[anything]` will be interpreted as Rich markup. 16→
session.assistant: Let me read a few representative docs and also look at an existing skill for the pattern.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: TUI Architecture Overview 3→read_when: 4→ - "understanding TUI structure" 5→ - "implementing TUI components" 6→ - "working with TUI data providers" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "reusing same DOM element id across loading/empty/content states" 9→ warning: "query_one() returns wrong element silently when id is reused across lifecycle phases. Use unique IDs per phase." 10→ - action: "calling widget methods directly from @work(thread=True) background threads" 11→ warning: "Direct widget calls from background threads cause silent UI corruption. Must use self.app.call_from_thread(callback, ...)." 12→ - action: "extending PlanDataProvider ABC" 13→ warning: "Requires 3-file update: abc.py + real.py + fake.py. Fake must initialize new dict in __init__. Missing init causes AttributeError at test time." 14→ - action: "adding a DataTable column with add_column(key=...)" 15→ warning: "Column key is a data binding contract — must match data field name. Silent failure when mismatched." 16→ - action: "moving @on decorated event handlers to a mixin" 17→ warning: "Textual's _MessagePumpMeta only scans class.__dict__, not inherited methods. Event handlers on mixins are silently ignored." 18→ - action: "adding CI check formatting to TUI screens" 19→ warning: "Use src/erk/tui/formatting/ci_checks.py shared module (format_check_line, format_summary_blockquote, format_check_runs) instead of duplicating formatting logic." 20→last_audited: "2026-02-17 18:30 PT" 21→audit_result: clean 22→--- 23→ 24→# TUI Architecture Overview 25→ 26→The erk TUI is built with Textual and follows a layered architecture separating data fetching, filtering, and rendering. 27→ 28→## Directory Structure 29→ 30→```
... [178 lines omitted] ...
128→Use fake providers instead of mocking. `FakePlanDataProvider` lives in `tests.fakes.gateway.plan_data_provider` and accepts keyword-only arguments (`plans`, `clipboard`, `browser`, `repo_root`, `fetch_error`). The `make_plan_row()` helper in the same module creates test `PlanRowData` instances with sensible defaults.

1→--- 2→title: Adding Commands to TUI 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new command to the TUI command palette" 5→ - "implementing TUI actions with streaming output" 6→ - "understanding the dual-handler pattern for TUI commands" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "generating TUI commands that depend on optional PlanRowData fields" 9→ warning: "Implement three-layer validation: registry predicate, handler guard, app-level helper. Never rely on registry predicate alone." 10→last_audited: "2026-02-05 00:00 PT" 11→audit_result: edited 12→--- 13→ 14→# Adding Commands to TUI 15→ 16→## Architecture: The Dual-Handler Pattern 17→ 18→Commands follow a **dual-handler pattern**: 19→ 20→1. **Registry** (`src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py`) — Defines `CommandDefinition` with metadata and availability predicates 21→2. **Handlers** — Implement actual logic in two locations: 22→ - `ErkDashApp.execute_palette_command()` in `app.py` — Main list context 23→ - `PlanDetailScreen.execute_command()` — Detail modal context 24→ 25→Commands need different behavior depending on invocation context. See the actual source files for current command definitions and handler implementations. 26→ 27→## Adding a New Command 28→ 29→1. **Add `CommandDefinition`** to `registry.py` with id, name, description, category, shortcut, availability predicate 30→2. **Add handler** in `ErkDashApp.execute_palette_command()` for main list context
... [69 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Textual API Quirks and Workarounds 3→read_when: 4→ - "working with Textual TUI framework" 5→ - "debugging DataTable, App, or CSS issues in Textual" 6→ - "writing tests for Textual applications" 7→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:30 PT" 8→audit_result: edited 9→--- 10→ 11→# Textual API Quirks and Workarounds 12→ 13→This document captures API quirks discovered while building the erk dash TUI with Textual. 14→ 15→## DataTable Quirks 16→ 17→### cursor_type Must Be Set via `__init__` 18→ 19→**Problem**: `cursor_type` is a `Reactive[CursorType]`, not a plain attribute. Setting it as a class attribute causes type errors: 20→ 21→```python 22→# WRONG - causes type error 23→class MyTable(DataTable): 24→ cursor_type = "row" # Type error: Literal['row'] not assignable to Reactive[CursorType] 25→``` 26→ 27→**Solution**: Pass to `super().__init__()`: 28→ 29→```python 30→# CORRECT
... [207 lines omitted] ...
80→**Problem**: The base `App.action_quit` is an async method. Overriding it with a sync method causes pyright errors about incompatible return types. 86→def action_quit(self) -> None: # Type error: incompatible override 104→**Problem**: When loading data in a background thread with `run_in_executor`, you might try to use `call_from_thread` to update the UI. This fails in test context with "must run in different thread" error. 159→# WRONG - causes MarkupError at render time 163→**Error signature:** 166→MarkupError: Expected markup value (found '://github.com/...') 196→**Problem**: When displaying raw JSON, code, or file contents in a Static widget, `escape_markup()` may not fully prevent `MarkupError`. Content with Python type annotations like `list[str]` or complex nested escape sequences can still cause parsing failures. 198→**Error signature:** 201→MarkupError: Expected markup value (found '\\"number\\"],\\n ...') 221→**Problem**: The StatusBar widget displays subprocess error messages that may contain brackets (e.g., `[error details]`). With default `markup=True`, these brackets are parsed as Rich markup tags, causing `MarkupError` at render time.

session.assistant: Let me look at an existing skill's structure for reference, and check the total size of the TUI docs.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→name: fake-driven-testing 3→description: 4→ This skill should be used when writing tests, fixing bugs, adding features, 5→ or modifying the gateway layer. Use when you need guidance on testing architecture, 6→ working with fakes, implementing ABC gateway interfaces, or understanding the defense-in-depth 7→ testing strategy. Essential for maintaining test quality and understanding where 8→ different types of tests belong. 9→--- 10→ 11→# Fake-Driven Testing Architecture for Python 12→ 13→**Use this skill when**: Writing tests, fixing bugs, adding features, or modifying gateway layers in Python projects. 14→ 15→**Prerequisites**: For Python code standards, load the `dignified-python-313` skill first. This skill focuses on testing architecture, not Python syntax. 16→ 17→## Overview 18→ 19→This skill provides a **defense-in-depth testing strategy** with five layers for Python applications: 20→ 21→``` 22→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 23→│ Layer 5 "smoke": Business Logic Integration Tests (5%) │ ← Smoke tests over real system 24→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ 25→│ Layer 4 "logic": Business Logic Tests (70%) │ ← Tests over fakes (MOST TESTS) 26→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ 27→│ Layer 3 "pure": Pure Unit Tests (10%) │ ← Zero dependencies, isolated testing 28→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ 29→│ Layer 2 "real-sanity": Integration Sanity Tests (10%) │ ← Fast validation with mocking 30→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
... [320 lines omitted] ...
162→- Error injection pattern 283→│ │ Purpose: Quick validation, catch syntax errors │ │

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: Count total lines in TUI + Textual docs

session.tool_result: 15 /Users/schrockn/code/erk/docs/learned/textual/tripwires.md 35 /Users/schrockn/code/erk/docs/learned/textual/widget-development.md 4265 total
references SKILL.md
session.assistant:
OK, here's what I see:
## Current State
- **37 TUI docs** (~3,600 lines) + **7 Textual docs** (~665 lines) = ~4,265 total lines across 44 files - Content covers: architecture, command patterns, data contracts, filtering, async patterns, modal screens, streaming, keyboard shortcuts, Textual framework quirks, etc. - 63 TUI tripwires + 1 Textual tripwire
## What a Skill Would Look Like
Looking at `fake-driven-testing` as the model, a TUI skill would have:
1. **`SKILL.md`** — Entry point with overview, quick decision routing ("What should I read?"), and key principles 2. **`references/`** — Distilled reference docs organized by workflow, not by original file
The key question is how to **compress and reorganize** 44 files into a skill. Here's my proposed structure:
### Proposed: `tui-development` skill
``` .claude/skills/tui-development/ ├── SKILL.md # Overview, architecture, decision routing └── references/ ├── architecture.md # Directory structure, data layers, mixin constraints ├── commands.md # Command registration, dual-handler, categories, palette ├── data-and-filtering.md # PlanRowData, PlanFilters, filter pipeline, sorting ├── async-and-streaming.md # Background workers, streaming output, thread safety ├── screens-and-widgets.md # Modals, tables, status bar, keyboard shortcuts ├── textual-quirks.md # Framework-specific gotchas (DataTable, CSS, Rich markup) └── tripwires.md # Consolidated tripwires (all 64) ```
### Key Decisions
1. **Merge tui/ and textual/ categories** — They're inseparable in practice; the Textual quirks are only relevant when working on the TUI 2. **Organize by workflow, not by original doc** — e.g., all command-related content (adding-commands, command-palette, command-execution, dual-handler, view-aware-commands, tui-command-registration, action-inventory) consolidates into one `commands.md` 3. **Keep tripwires as a dedicated reference** — They're machine-consumed for safety, worth preserving intact 4. **Drop some docs entirely** — Several docs are very narrow (title-truncation-edge-cases, stacked-pr-indicator, plan-title-rendering-pipeline) and can be folded into broader references or dropped if the code is self-documenting
### What I'd Want Your Input On
- **Should this replace the docs/learned/ files or coexist?** Skills are session-persistent context; docs/learned are grep-discoverable references. We could deprecate the individual docs or keep them as the "source of truth" that feeds the skill. - **Naming**: `tui-development` vs `tui` vs `erk-tui`? - **Scope**: Include the Textual quirks inline, or keep them as a separate reference?
Want me to proceed with drafting this, or do you want to adjust the structure first?
session.user: what would it look like if we wanted a textual-specific skill that an erk-tui skill would compose?
session.assistant:
Good question. The idea would be: `textual` captures framework-level knowledge (works for any Textual app), while `erk-tui` captures erk-specific patterns (our architecture, our data types, our commands).
Let me check what content falls cleanly into each bucket.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: Textual Background Workers 3→read_when: 4→ - "running subprocess in TUI without blocking" 5→ - "streaming output to Textual widgets" 6→ - "using run_worker for async operations" 7→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 8→audit_result: clean 9→--- 10→ 11→# Textual Background Workers 12→ 13→## When to Use `run_worker()` 14→ 15→Use `run_worker()` for I/O-bound operations that should not block the UI: 16→ 17→- Subprocess execution with output streaming 18→- Network requests 19→- File operations on large files 20→ 21→## Subprocess Streaming Pattern 22→ 23→```python 24→from textual.worker import Worker, WorkerState 25→import asyncio 26→ 27→def action_execute(self) -> None: 28→ """Start command execution.""" 29→ self._output_panel.visible = True 30→ self.run_worker(self._stream_subprocess, exclusive=True)
... [34 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Textual Widget Development Guide 3→read_when: 4→ - "creating Textual widgets" 5→ - "adding ModalScreen dialogs" 6→ - "implementing keyboard bindings" 7→ - "writing Textual CSS styles" 8→last_audited: "2026-02-05 00:00 PT" 9→audit_result: edited 10→--- 11→ 12→# Textual Widget Development 13→ 14→For standard Textual API patterns (ModalScreen, Bindings, compose(), CSS, widget types), consult the [Textual documentation](https://textual.textualize.io/). This doc focuses on erk-specific patterns. 15→ 16→## Erk TUI Source Layout 17→ 18→| Component | Location | 19→| --------- | ----------------------------------- | 20→| Main app | `src/erk/tui/app.py` (`ErkDashApp`) | 21→| Widgets | `src/erk/tui/widgets/` | 22→| Screens | `src/erk/tui/screens/` | 23→| CSS | `src/erk/tui/styles/` | 24→ 25→## Reference Implementations 26→ 27→- **ModalScreens**: `HelpScreen` (`screens/help_screen.py`), `PlanDetailScreen` (`screens/plan_detail_screen.py`), `IssueBodyScreen` (`screens/issue_body_screen.py`) 28→- **Streaming output**: `CommandOutputPanel` (`widgets/command_output.py`) — uses `RichLog` for live output display 29→- **Clickable links**: `widgets/clickable_link.py` — demonstrates accessing app properties from widgets 30→
... [6 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Textual TUI Testing Patterns 3→read_when: 4→ - "testing Textual TUI components" 5→ - "writing tests for ModalScreen" 6→ - "testing keyboard bindings" 7→ - "using Pilot API for async tests" 8→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 9→audit_result: clean 10→--- 11→ 12→# Textual TUI Testing Patterns 13→ 14→## Overview 15→ 16→This project uses Textual for TUI applications. Testing Textual apps requires async tests with the Pilot API. 17→ 18→## Basic Test Structure 19→ 20→```python 21→import pytest 22→from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanDetailScreen 23→ 24→@pytest.mark.asyncio 25→async def test_tui_behavior() -> None: 26→ """Test TUI interaction.""" 27→ provider = FakePlanDataProvider([make_plan_row(123, "Test")]) 28→ filters = PlanFilters.default() 29→ app = ErkDashApp(provider, filters, refresh_interval=0) 30→
... [85 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: DataTable Rich Markup Escaping 3→last_audited: "2026-02-17 18:30 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "adding cell values to Textual DataTable" 7→ - "displaying user data in DataTable cells" 8→ - "troubleshooting invisible text in DataTable" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "adding cell values to Textual DataTable" 11→ warning: "Always wrap in `Text(value)` if strings contain user data with brackets. Otherwise `[anything]` will be interpreted as Rich markup." 12→--- 13→ 14→# DataTable Rich Markup Escaping 15→ 16→Textual's DataTable interprets plain strings as Rich markup by default. This causes user data containing brackets (like `[erk-learn]` prefixes) to disappear. 17→ 18→## The Problem 19→ 20→When you pass a plain string to `DataTable.add_row()`, Rich interprets bracket sequences as markup tags: 21→ 22→```python 23→# WRONG: The [prefix] disappears because Rich interprets it as markup 24→table.add_row("[erk-learn] My Plan Title") 25→# Result: "My Plan Title" (prefix invisible) 26→``` 27→ 28→The `[erk-learn]` text is treated as a Rich style tag. Since no style named `erk-learn` exists, Rich silently renders nothing for that portion. 29→ 30→## The Solution
... [55 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: Now let me also quickly check some of the erk-specific TUI docs to see how they'd differ from the Textual layer.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: TUI Filter Pipeline Pattern 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new filter to the TUI dashboard" 5→ - "understanding how objective/stack/text filters interact" 6→ - "modifying the escape key behavior in the TUI" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding a new filter without updating the escape chain" 9→ warning: "New filter implementations must add an entry to `action_exit_app()` progressive escape chain. Missing entries leave filters stuck with no way for the user to clear them." 10→ - action: "changing filter application order in _apply_filter_and_sort()" 11→ warning: "Filter order is intentional: objective → stack → text → sort. Objective is broadest (cross-stack), stack is mid-level, text is narrowest. Changing order produces unexpected results." 12→ - action: "using a mutable set for _stack_filter_branches" 13→ warning: "Stack filter branches use frozenset[str] for immutability and efficient membership testing. Do not use set or list." 14→--- 15→ 16→# TUI Filter Pipeline Pattern 17→ 18→The TUI dashboard applies filters in a defined pipeline sequence: objective filter, stack filter, text filter, then sort. Each filter follows the same toggle pattern with state variables, toggle actions, and escape chain integration. 19→ 20→## Filter Pipeline 21→ 22→`_apply_filter_and_sort()` in `src/erk/tui/app.py` applies filters in sequence: 23→ 24→1. **Objective filter**: Filters rows matching `_objective_filter_issue` 25→2. **Stack filter**: Filters rows matching branches in `_stack_filter_branches` 26→3. **Text filter**: Applies text search query if `FilterMode.ACTIVE` 27→4. **Sort**: Sorts using `SortKey` (supports `BranchActivity` ordering) 28→ 29→## State Variables 30→
... [48 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Textual CommandPalette Guide 3→read_when: 4→ - "implementing command palette in Textual TUI" 5→ - "hiding system commands from command palette" 6→ - "get_system_commands method" 7→ - "removing Keys Quit Screenshot Theme from palette" 8→ - "adding emoji prefixes to command palette entries" 9→ - "using CommandCategory for command categorization" 10→last_audited: "2026-02-05 00:00 PT" 11→audit_result: edited 12→--- 13→ 14→# Textual CommandPalette Guide 15→ 16→For standard Textual CommandPalette patterns (Providers, Hit/DiscoveryHit, Matcher, async generators, Screen.COMMANDS), consult the [Textual documentation](https://textual.textualize.io/). This doc focuses on erk-specific patterns and a critical Textual pitfall. 17→ 18→## System Commands Pitfall 19→ 20→**CRITICAL:** `get_system_commands` must be overridden on the **App class**, not the Screen class. Textual calls `app.get_system_commands(screen)` when opening the palette — it never calls this method on screens. 21→ 22→```python 23→# WRONG — This method is never called by Textual 24→class MyModalScreen(ModalScreen): 25→ def get_system_commands(self, screen: Screen) -> Iterator[SystemCommand]: 26→ return iter(()) # Has no effect! 27→ 28→# CORRECT — Override on App class 29→class MyApp(App): 30→ def get_system_commands(self, screen: Screen) -> Iterator[SystemCommand]:
... [40 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Now I have a clear picture. Here's how the two-skill composition would work:
## Proposed Two-Skill Structure
### Skill 1: `textual` — Framework Knowledge (portable, not erk-specific)
**Audience**: Anyone building a Textual app. No erk imports or paths.
``` .claude/skills/textual/ ├── SKILL.md # Overview, when to use, decision routing └── references/ ├── quirks.md # API gotchas (DataTable, App, CSS, Rich markup) ├── async-patterns.md # Background workers, run_worker, call_from_thread, thread safety └── testing.md # Pilot API, async test structure, pauses, screen stack assertions ```
**Content sourced from**: - `textual/quirks.md` → `quirks.md` (almost verbatim — it's already framework-generic) - `textual/background-workers.md` → `async-patterns.md` - `textual/testing.md` → `testing.md` (strip erk-specific fixtures, keep Pilot patterns) - `textual/datatable-markup-escaping.md` → fold into `quirks.md` (it's a DataTable gotcha) - `textual/widget-development.md` → drop (already says "consult Textual docs", mostly just links to erk stuff) - `tui/command-palette.md` → the `get_system_commands` pitfall goes into `quirks.md`; the erk-specific command categories go into erk-tui - `tui/textual-async.md` → fold into `async-patterns.md`
**Tripwires** (~5): DataTable markup escaping, `call_from_thread` requirement, `_render()` reserved name, `_filters` attribute collision, `prevent_default()` + `stop()` pattern.
### Skill 2: `erk-tui` — Erk Dashboard Development
**Prerequisite**: "For Textual framework patterns, load the `textual` skill first."
``` .claude/skills/erk-tui/ ├── SKILL.md # Architecture overview, directory structure, decision routing └── references/ ├── data-layer.md # PlanRowData, PlanFilters, PlanDataProvider ABC, data contract ├── commands.md # Registration, dual-handler, categories, action inventory, view-aware filtering ├── filtering-and-views.md # Filter pipeline, toggle pattern, escape chain, view switching, view configs ├── screens-and-widgets.md # Modals, PlanDataTable, status bar, keyboard shortcuts, column patterns ├── async-operations.md # Streaming output, subprocess feedback, multi-operation tracking, state snapshots └── tripwires.md # All 63 erk-tui tripwires ```
**Content sourced from** (mapping the 37 tui/ docs):
| erk-tui reference | Source docs | |---|---| | `data-layer.md` | architecture (data section), data-contract, plan-row-data, derived-display-columns, frozen-dataclass-field-management, column-addition-pattern, dashboard-columns | | `commands.md` | adding-commands, action-inventory, tui-command-registration, dual-handler-pattern, view-aware-commands, command-execution, clipboard-text-generation | | `filtering-and-views.md` | filter-pipeline, filter-toggle-pattern, view-switching, view-mode-help | | `screens-and-widgets.md` | modal-screen-pattern, modal-widget-embedding, one-shot-prompt-modal, keyboard-shortcuts, status-indicators, lifecycle-display, stacked-pr-indicator, plan-title-rendering-pipeline, title-truncation-edge-cases, runs-tab-architecture | | `async-operations.md` | streaming-output, subprocess-feedback, multi-operation-tracking, async-action-refresh-pattern, async-state-snapshot | | `SKILL.md` | architecture (structure, mixins, design principles) |
### The Composition Boundary
The split is clean because it follows a natural seam:
| Question | Answer from... | |---|---| | "How do I use `call_from_thread`?" | `textual` skill | | "How do I update the status bar from a worker thread?" | `erk-tui` skill (references `textual` for the underlying pattern) | | "Why is my DataTable cell empty?" | `textual` skill (markup escaping) | | "How do I add a column to PlanDataTable?" | `erk-tui` skill | | "How do I test a ModalScreen?" | `textual` skill (Pilot API) | | "How do I test the confirmation dialog?" | `erk-tui` skill (FakePlanDataProvider setup) |
### What This Buys You
1. **`textual` skill is reusable** — if you ever build another Textual app, the framework knowledge carries over 2. **`erk-tui` stays focused** — it's all about erk's specific architecture, not framework mechanics 3. **Smaller context per skill** — an agent working on a simple Textual widget quirk doesn't need 63 erk tripwires loaded 4. **Clear prerequisite chain** — `erk-tui` SKILL.md says "load `textual` first", similar to how `fake-driven-testing` says "load `dignified-python` first"
### Open Question
The `textual` skill would be relatively small (~3 reference files). That's fine — `dignified-python` has a subprocess.md that's just one file too. But worth asking: is there enough Textual-generic content to justify the split, or would it be simpler to have one `erk-tui` skill with a `textual-quirks.md` reference inside it?
Want me to start building these out?
session.user:
i think it should one skill called erk-tui
what would be alterantives ? e.g erk-dev-tui, erk-arch-tui, etc, list 10 or so
session.assistant:
1. `erk-tui` 2. `erk-tui-dev` 3. `erk-dash` 4. `erk-dash-dev` 5. `erk-tui-architecture` 6. `erk-tui-guide` 7. `erk-tui-patterns` 8. `erk-dashboard` 9. `tui-development` 10. `erk-textual`
My recommendation is `erk-tui` — it's the shortest, most obvious name, and matches how the codebase already refers to it (`src/erk/tui/`, "TUI" in doc titles, `erk dash` as the command). The others either add unnecessary qualifiers or are less discoverable.
session.user: ok i would also want to use this as time to cleanup and crystallize the content AND to have to learning system be aware of the skill so we do not re-add the same content again
session.assistant:
Good — three goals:
1. **Create `erk-tui` skill** — consolidate 44 docs into a well-structured skill 2. **Clean up content** — deduplicate, remove stale info, crystallize what matters 3. **Make the learning system aware** — so future `/erk:learn` runs route TUI insights to the skill instead of recreating docs/learned files
Let me plan this properly. First, let me understand how the learning system currently works and how existing skills integrate with it.

session.tool_result:
Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me create a summary report for the user:
## Summary: Learned-Docs Skill and Documentation System
I've thoroughly explored the learned-docs skill and the erk documentation system. Here are the key findings:
### **1. How Docs/Learned Files Are Created and Maintained**
**Source Files:** - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.claude/skills/learned-docs/SKILL.md` — The skill documentation - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.claude/skills/learned-docs/learned-docs-core.md` — Content quality standards (the "cornerstone")
**Frontmatter Requirements:** Every doc (except index.md) MUST have: ```yaml --- title: Document Title read_when: - "first condition" - "second condition" --- ```
**Key Rules from learned-docs-core.md:** - Docs are token caches for AI agents, not human documentation - Document reality, not aspiration (including tech debt and workarounds) - Never reproduce source code (4 exceptions: data formats, third-party APIs, anti-patterns, input/output examples) - Explain WHY, not WHAT — the "what" is in source code - Cross-cutting insight that spans multiple files is the sweet spot
... [111 lines omitted] ...
warning: "Load `skill-name` skill first. See [full-doc](../path/) for context."

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me compile my findings into a clear report:
## Summary
Based on my thorough analysis of the erk learning system, here's how the documentation routing and categorization works:
### **1. How `/erk:learn` Decides Where to Put Documentation**
The learning system **does NOT have explicit routing logic built into `/erk:learn`** itself. Instead, it follows a **file-path-based categorization system**:
- **Category is determined by directory structure**, not by content inspection - When a document is placed at `docs/learned/architecture/foo.md`, it's automatically categorized as "architecture" - Root-level docs at `docs/learned/foo.md` are categorized as "uncategorized" - The `erk docs sync` command then regenerates index files based purely on **where files physically exist**
**Reference**: `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/agent_docs/operations.py` lines 407-419: ```python def _get_category_from_path(doc_path: str) -> str | None: """Extract category from a doc path.""" parts = doc_path.split("/") if len(parts) > 1: return parts[0] return None ```
### **2. Awareness of Skills and Routing to Them**
The learning system **is aware of skills but does NOT automatically route content to them**:
- The `existing-docs-checker` agent **actively searches** `.claude/skills/` alongside `docs/learned/` to prevent duplicates
... [84 lines omitted] ...

Perfect! I now have comprehensive information. Let me compile the complete findings for the user.
## Summary: Erk Skill Onboarding and Registration System
I've thoroughly analyzed the erk-skill-onboarding skill and the entire skill registration system. Here's a comprehensive breakdown:
### Core Documentation
**Main skill reference:** `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.claude/skills/erk-skill-onboarding/SKILL.md`
This file provides a complete decision tree and checklist for adding new skills. It's well-documented and serves as the primary guide.
---
### Skill Registration: Three Key Decisions
Every skill must answer these three questions:
1. **Bundled or Unbundled?** - **Bundled** = distributed externally via `erk init capability add <name>` (installed to other projects) - **Unbundled** = lives only in `.claude/skills/` within the erk repo (erk-internal use)
2. **Codex Portable or Claude-Only?** - **Codex Portable** = works with any AI agent (no Claude-specific features) - **Claude-Only** = uses hooks, session logs, slash commands, Claude Code features
3. **Required or Optional?** (bundled skills only) - **Required** = auto-installed by `erk init` - **Optional** = user opts in via `erk init capability add`
... [209 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Here's what I learned from the three explorations:
## Current State of Affairs
1. **No skill redirect mechanism exists** — The learning system always outputs to `docs/learned/`. The `existing-docs-checker` agent detects skill content for deduplication but can't route new insights to a skill.
2. **Skill registration** — For an unbundled (erk-internal) skill, we need: - `.claude/skills/erk-tui/SKILL.md` + references - Add to `_UNBUNDLED_SKILLS` in `src/erk/capabilities/skills/bundled.py`
3. **No deprecation pattern exists** — There's no documented way to say "this docs/learned category is now covered by a skill."
## Proposed Plan
### Phase 1: Create the `erk-tui` skill - Consolidate 44 docs into the skill structure we discussed - Include Textual quirks as a reference file within the skill
### Phase 2: Wire it into the system - Register in `_UNBUNDLED_SKILLS` - Update AGENTS.md routing table: `**TUI code** → erk-tui skill` - Add to the "Load these skills FIRST" section
### Phase 3: Make the learning system aware This is the novel part. Two approaches:
**Option A: Category redirect marker** — Add a new convention to `docs/learned/tui/index.md` and `docs/learned/textual/index.md` that the learning pipeline reads:
```yaml --- skill_redirect: erk-tui skill_redirect_note: "TUI content is maintained in the erk-tui skill. New insights should update the skill, not create docs here." --- ```
Then update the `existing-docs-checker` agent and/or `plan-synthesizer` agent to recognize this marker and route accordingly.
**Option B: Delete the categories entirely** — Remove `docs/learned/tui/` and `docs/learned/textual/` after migrating content. The learning system can't create files in categories that don't exist (it only writes to existing categories or root). Add a tripwire in the skill itself.
**Option C: Tombstone files** — Replace each category with a single redirect file that points to the skill, keeping the directory alive but empty of real content.
My recommendation is **Option A + partial cleanup**: Keep a slim `docs/learned/tui/index.md` with the redirect marker and maybe 2-3 docs that are genuinely "reference-only" (like the tripwires file which is auto-generated). Delete the rest. This way the learning pipeline knows where to route, and we don't lose the category for future use.
Want me to proceed with this plan, or do you want to adjust the approach to the learning system integration?
session.user: well i still want to develop *raw* content in the learned folder, and then have raw insights be harvested into more curated content in the skill
session.assistant:
Ah, that's a much better mental model. The docs/learned files are the **raw material** — where insights get captured in the moment — and the skill is the **refined product** that gets curated from them over time.
So the flow is:
``` Session insights → /erk:learn → docs/learned/tui/*.md (raw, append-only-ish) ↓ periodic curation / harvest ↓ .claude/skills/erk-tui/ (crystallized, maintained) ```
This means:
1. **Keep `docs/learned/tui/` and `docs/learned/textual/` alive** — they continue receiving new raw insights from the learning pipeline as-is 2. **The skill is a curated distillation** — not a 1:1 migration but a hand-edited synthesis 3. **The learning system doesn't need to change routing** — it keeps writing to docs/learned. The new thing is a **harvest step** that periodically pulls raw insights into the skill 4. **Tripwires stay in both places** — raw docs have them for discovery, the skill has a consolidated version for when it's loaded
### What changes vs. my earlier proposal
- **Don't delete docs/learned files** — they're the living raw layer - **Don't add redirect markers** — the categories stay active - **Do add a harvest mechanism** — a command or workflow that reviews raw docs against the skill and identifies what's new/changed - **Do update AGENTS.md** — so agents load the skill for TUI work (the curated version) rather than grep-reading 44 raw files
### Concrete deliverables
1. **Create `.claude/skills/erk-tui/`** — curated skill distilled from current raw content 2. **Register it** — `_UNBUNDLED_SKILLS`, AGENTS.md routing 3. **Add a harvest command** (or at minimum document the harvest workflow) — "compare docs/learned/tui/*.md against skill references, identify unincorporated insights" 4. **Tag raw docs** — optional: add frontmatter like `curated_in: erk-tui` so future harvest knows which docs have been processed
Does this match what you're thinking? If so, I'll start with creating the skill content — that's the biggest piece.
Let me explore the remaining pieces I need to understand before creating the plan.

Also note any content that seems stale or low-value enough to drop.
